![Robert Wood Johnson Foundation](/content/dam/rwjf-components-guide/RWJF-Logo.svg)
![Robert Wood Johnson Foundation](/content/dam/rwjf-components-guide/RWJF-Logo.svg)

# Transformational Community Engagement to Advance Health Equity

![A man speak ingwith community leaders and activists.](https://rwjf.scene7.com/is/image/rwjf/RWJF2019_COH_Greenville_VanAuken_251865?ts=1740616247989&dpr=off)

#### Community engagement is one of the ways states are attempting to establish and maintain trust while improving the accessibility and quality of healthcare services for everyone.

#### The Issue

Community engagement is central to addressing the systemic inequities and structural discrimination entrenched in health systems. By fostering trust and mutual respect, exposing unforeseen or unintended barriers to health, and improving program efficacy by accounting for the experiences of the people impacted by programs and policies, community engagement can promote equity.

### Key Findings

#### Conclusion

Reflecting on current community engagement initiatives and infrastructure is the first step in moving towards transformational community engagement. To do this, state agencies must identify where their organization’s existing community engagement efforts fall in the context of health equity. Once a benchmark is established, the next step is evaluating agency resources and setting goals to advance equitable community engagement.

A related brief, also available for download, shares two case studies from Colorado and Virginia that both highlight real world examples of how states can invest in coordinated community engagement strategies to amplify the voices of those directly impacted by healthcare policy decisions and drive improvements.

#### About State Health and Value Strategies

State Health and Value Strategies (SHVS) assists states in their efforts to transform health and healthcare by providing targeted technical assistance to state officials and agencies. The program is a grantee of the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation, led by staff at Princeton University’s School of Public and International Affairs. The program connects states with experts and peers to undertake healthcare transformation initiatives. By engaging state officials, the program provides lessons learned, highlights successful strategies, and brings together states with experts in the field. Learn more at *[www.shvs.org](http://www.shvs.org)*.

#### About Health Equity Solutions

Health Equity Solutions (HES) promotes policies, programs, and practices that result in equitable health care access, delivery, and outcomes for all people regardless of race or income. HES works with State Health and Value Strategies (SHVS) to guide the program’s health equity work generally while also providing targeted technical assistance to states. HES is based in Hartford, Connecticut and focuses its work outside of the support it provides to SHVS on achieving health equity in Connecticut. Learn more at [*www.hesct.org*](https://www.hesct.org/ "Opens in a new window").

## Utility Nav

## Get Started Exploring Resources

# Collection Community Engagement Interventions to Improve Outcomes

![](/sites/default/files/Community-Engagement-Collection_Banner.jpg)
![](/sites/default/files/styles/embedded_full/public/Community-Engagement-Collection_Banner.jpg?itok=vv1xgwOt)

##### What is a Collection?

Playbook Collections explore interventions that address the needs of people with complex needs by curating available peer-reviewed research, case studies, and tools. Although not exhaustive, Collections evolve as new and promising research and other resources are available.

Across the nation, health care payers, providers, and policymakers are seeking ways to engage community members to better understand barriers to good health and invest in programs that better meet community needs. Medicaid enrollees and people enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid disproportionately experience high rates of complex health and social needs. Engaging with this population can positively impact health outcomes.

While there is [robust evidence](https://bettercareplaybook.org/resources/patient-engagement-strategies-adults-chronic-conditions-evidence-map) supporting the benefits of patient engagement in improving self-management for people with complex needs, there is limited evidence on engaging patients and community members in organizational or policy decision-making and co-design. Driven by growing recognition that involving community members in this way can improve population health, many health care stakeholders are seeking to enhance their community engagement processes. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services’ [Access rule](https://www.cms.gov/newsroom/fact-sheets/ensuring-access-medicaid-services-final-rule-cms-2442-f), published in April 2024, aims to improve health outcomes for beneficiaries by expanding opportunities for people with lived experience to inform Medicaid program design and policy decision-making.

Health care stakeholders, including policymakers, health plans, and providers, can use this *Better Care Playbook* Collection to understand emerging evidence and best practices for implementing community engagement interventions to better meet community needs and improve health outcomes.

# What are emerging evidence and best practices for designing community engagement strategies in health care?

Following are summaries of peer-reviewed research and reports on community engagement approaches. Many of these resources summarize qualitative data on process outcomes and detail promising approaches for improving community engagement initiatives, including at the policy and program levels.

# [Changes in How Medicaid Engages Individuals with Lived Experience: Resource Roundup](/_blog/2024/18/changes-how-medicaid-engages-individuals-lived-experience-resource-roundup)

# [Strategies for Meaningfully Engaging MassHealth Members to Inform Program and Policy Decisions](/resources/strategies-meaningfully-engaging-masshealth-members-inform-program-and-policy-decisions) (Blue Cross Blue Shield of Massachusetts Foundation)

# [Building Trust: Leadership Reflections on Community Empowerment and Engagement in a Large Urban Initiative](/resources/building-trust-leadership-reflections-community-empowerment-and-engagement-large-urban) (BMC Public Health)

# [Community-Engaged Healthcare Model for Currently Under-Served Individuals Involved in the Healthcare System](/resources/community-engaged-healthcare-model-currently-under-served-individuals-involved-healthcare) (SSM Population Health )

# [Leveraging Community Expertise to Advance Health Equity](/resources/leveraging-community-expertise-advance-health-equity) (Urban Institute)

# [Exploring Consumer Engagement Experiences: A Research Project Co-Led by the National Consumer Scholars](/resources/exploring-consumer-engagement-experiences-research-project-co-led-national-consumer) (The Camden Coalition)

# [Engaging Consumers in Medicaid Program Design: Strategies from the States](/resources/engaging-consumers-medicaid-program-design-strategies-states) (Milbank Quarterly)

# [Key Findings From the Medicaid MCO Learning Hub Discussion Group Series and Roundtable — Focus on Member Engagement and the Consumer Voice](/resources/key-findings-medicaid-mco-learning-hub-discussion-group-series-and-roundtable-focus) (NORC)

# What does effective community engagement look like in practice?

Following are first-hand perspectives and experiences on implementing community engagement initiatives that drive health care system improvements. Detailed case studies can help inform stakeholders interested in implementing similar approaches.

# [Real Voices, Real Impact: “The People Say” Connects Older Adults’ Lived Experience to Policy Action](/_blog/2025/6/real-voices-real-impact-people-say-connects-older-adults-lived-experience-policy)

# [Comprehensive Member and Community Engagement: A Multi-Tiered Approach by a Safety Net Health Plan](/_blog/2024/3/comprehensive-member-and-community-engagement-multi-tiered-approach-safety-net-health)

# [How Community Engagement Can Support Whole-Person Primary Care](/resources/how-community-engagement-can-support-whole-person-primary-care) (Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality)

# [An Embedded Approach to Engaging Community Members: Lessons from Southcentral Foundation’s Nuka System of Care](/_blog/2023/18/embedded-approach-engaging-community-members-lessons-southcentral-foundations-nuka)

# [Making Addiction Treatment More Equitable for Black People by Engaging People with Lived Experience of Substance Use Disorders](/_blog/2023/27/making-addiction-treatment-more-equitable-black-people-engaging-people-lived)

# [Cocreating Evidence-Informed Health Equity Policy with Community](/resources/cocreating-evidence-informed-health-equity-policy-community) (Health Services Research)

# [State Examples of Medicaid Community Engagement Strategies: Two Case Studies](/resources/state-examples-medicaid-community-engagement-strategies-two-case-studies) (State Health & Value Strategies)

# [Involving People with Lived Experience in Complex Care Research](/_blog/2023/3/involving-people-lived-experience-complex-care-research)

# How can my organization implement similar community engagement approaches?

Following are practical tools that health plans, policymakers, health system leaders, and other interested stakeholders can use to develop community engagement initiatives that foster program, organization, or policy change and center community voice.

# [The People Say](/resources/people-say) (The SCAN Foundation and The Public Policy Lab)

# [Making the Case for Engaging People with Lived Experience and Expertise in State Behavioral Health Reforms](/resources/making-case-engaging-people-lived-experience-and-expertise-state-behavioral-health) (Center for Health Care Strategies)

# [A Roadmap for Effective Community Engagement in Healthcare: Final Report from INSPIRE Phase I](/resources/roadmap-effective-community-engagement-healthcare-final-report-inspire-phase-i) (Camden Coalition)

# [Engaging Community Members: A Guide to Equitable Compensation](/resources/engaging-community-members-guide-equitable-compensation) (Center for Health Care Strategies)

# [Community Member Engagement Resource Center](/resources/community-member-engagement-resource-center) (Center for Health Care Strategies)

# [Building Partnerships with Community Members to Advance Public Health](/resources/building-partnerships-community-members-advance-public-health) (Camden Coalition)

# [Medi-Cal Member Advisory Committee: Design Recommendations for the California Department of Health Care Services](/resources/medi-cal-member-advisory-committee-design-recommendations-california-department-health) (California Health Care Foundation)

# [Shifting the Power Balance: Creating Health System Accountability through Trusted Community Partnerships](/resources/shifting-power-balance-creating-health-system-accountability-through-trusted-community) (Center for Health Care Strategies)

# [Methods and Emerging Strategies to Engage People with Lived Experience](/resources/methods-and-emerging-strategies-engage-people-lived-experience) (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services)

# [Community Engagement Toolkit: A Participatory Action Approach Towards Health Equity and Justice](/resources/community-engagement-toolkit-participatory-action-approach-towards-health-equity-and) (Public Health Institute)

# Community engagement: a health promotion guide for universal health coverage in the hands of the people ﻿

![Community engagement: a health promotion guide for universal health coverage in the hands of the people ﻿](https://iris.who.int/bitstream/handle/10665/334379/9789240010529-eng.pdf.jpg?sequence=8)

### Overview

WHO has defined community engagement as “a process of developing relationships that enable stakeholders to work together to address health-related issues and promote well-being to achieve positive health impact and outcomes”. There are undeniable benefits to engaging communities in promoting health and wellbeing. At its core, community engagement enables changes in behaviour, environments, policies, programmes and practices within communities. There are different levels, depths and breadths of community engagement which determine the type and degree of involvement of the people.

This guide is intended for change agents involved in community work at the level of communities and healthy settings.

![](/ResourcePackages/WHO/assets/icons/cc-icons/cc.svg)
![](/ResourcePackages/WHO/assets/icons/cc-icons/by.svg)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Partnering with local organizations fosters shared resources and knowledge, enhancing community health initiatives and addressing specific health disparities effectively.
Engaging community members ensures their voices are heard, leading to interventions that are relevant and tailored to their unique health challenges and cultural contexts.
Customizing health interventions based on community assessment data helps address specific barriers, promoting better health outcomes and greater program acceptance.
Establishing open communication about goals and processes enhances trust, empowering communities to participate actively in their health care journey.
Utilizing existing local services and assets maximizes efficiency and sustainability, ensuring interventions are grounded in the community's strengths and capabilities.
